costs: Political friction with organized gimnázium-director interest groups who have demonstrated direct access to top leadership (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
Risk of parental backlash given free-choice framing as non-negotiable (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03)
Administrative capacity needed to manage differentiated caps across urban/rural schools
content.equity_impact: Reduces the absolute scale of segregation-generating capacity in the state sector, but risk of migration to unregulated church/private selective capacity could neutralize net equity gains unless paired with parallel constraints on those sectors.
expected_benefits: Reduced state-sector selective-track share without requiring parliamentary legislation
Uses existing discretionary and threshold-setting tools already validated by precedent (Tiszavasvári case)
Aligns capacity contraction with genuine demographic decline, reducing political framing as ideologically motivated abolition
content.goal: Shrink the state-sector footprint of the selective track by tightening admission thresholds and capacity allocation, without altering admission criteria for remaining places.

implementation_steps.action: Set nationwide capacity ceiling for state-sector 6/8-year gimnázium intake, tied to declining cohort projections
Apply threshold increases and capacity caps at flagship oversubscribed schools first
Monitor displacement of rejected applicants into 4-year gimnázium or vocational tracks and adjust caps to avoid unintended vocational-track overflow

mechanisms: Ministry already exercises discretionary approval power to deny new 6/8-year track requests on segregation grounds, demonstrating an existing administrative lever for capacity control without new legislation (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09, F-live-political_feasibility-08).
Recommended entry thresholds (70/100) already function as a de facto capacity-limiting tool, and raising them further would mechanically shrink the admitted pool (F-live-school_network_planning-04).

Physical capacity has already stagnated at ~25,962 students despite oversubscription, showing a passive capacity ceiling exists that active policy could formalize and extend (F-live-school_network_planning-03).

State-sector jurisdiction covers Klebelsberg Centre and its 60 district sub-units but explicitly excludes church, foundation, and private schools, meaning contraction levers are legally partial (F-live-legal_and_governance-07).
risks: Church and private schools, exempt from state capacity caps, could absorb displaced demand and expand their already-growing selective share, offsetting state-sector contraction (F-live-implementation_planning-05, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
Threshold increases could push marginal students into vocational tracks involuntarily, a concern already raised by parent advocacy groups (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
Contraction without addressing underlying admission criteria leaves selection mechanism's inequity-per-seat unchanged (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
content.title: Capacity-capped contraction of state-sector 6/8-year places via demographic-linked thresholds
